title: What people who shun eps 25 and 26 are missing
display_title: What people who shun eps 25 and 26 are missing
wikitext:
[]: What people who shun eps. 25 & 26 are missing.             Quote Post by Misato's toyboy » Sun Jun 07, 2009 2:42 pm There are fans &/or purists who shun & don't watch the TV episodes 25 & 26, instead preferring 25' & 26', which are what make up End of Evangelion. I admit to being a fan of the original ending, (& have my own theory for how all can be slotted together). What those who don't watch 25 & 26 are missing, is..not..action, but revelations of Shinji's, Asuka's, Misato's, Rei's psyches, & what Gendou & Ritsuko each really want. 25 & 26 are stage theatre scenes of higher intensity - in an almost Victorian theatre way, & probably more than the average teen anime watcher could/can follow or swallow. The personality insights not only show what's eating up Eva's main characters inside & their mental states, but how their feelings of incompleteness & personal histories have made them what they are. On a lighter note, there is the alternate & fun Evaverse scene, which resulted in video games & the Angelic Days manga. Taking the ending to 26, & therefore Eva TV on its own, Shinji (seemingly) accepts Complementation. We are all reunited, melded, into one theoretically happier corporeal & mental being, with no personalities. Surely for the happier TV Shinji, that is better than the miserable individuality which is what EoE Shinji chooses? And isn't the Complementation hive-togetherness credo more Japanese, so more in his national upbringing?

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Sun Jun 07, 2009 3:04 pm Misato's Toyboy wrote: Taking the ending to 26, & therefore Eva TV on its own, Shinji (seemingly) accepts Complementation.

[]:             Quote Post by Axell » Sun Jun 07, 2009 4:11 pm Sailor Star Dust wrote: Everything that i could say along with the same opinions

[]:             Quote Post by LeoXiao » Sun Jun 07, 2009 4:28 pm EoTV was a kinda weak ending, simply because it didn't bring the plot ends together very well, but for me it had the effect of making me not really care about the plot being ended perfectly, since everything was confusing anyway and that what makes the series great, IMO, are the complex character interactions and developments, which didn't require such a strongly structured plot. EoE, in my view, is essential viewing material since to not watch it would be disrespectful to the creators, and also because it makes the psyches of the characters more clear, in addition to properly ending the whole thing. Though I personally think that EoTV was a very, very abstract version of Instrumentality (minus the JSSDF battle except Misato and Ritsuko's deaths), and by no means an "alternate ending" or some crap like that, to debate about the issue from that perspective seems meaningless to me.

[]:             Quote Post by BrikHaus » Sun Jun 07, 2009 4:51 pm Episodes 25 and 26 are the only good episodes. Really, people would be best served only watching those two, and forgoing everything else.

[]:             Quote Post by BattleMonkey » Sun Jun 07, 2009 5:06 pm I always see the TV episodes to simply be more insight into the characters and events that were portrayed in EoE. I never understood the claim that in the TV version Shinji accepts complementation.... that's completely against what is said at the end of 26 TV episode where he accepts himself for HIMSELF. He accepted it originally with the instrumentality, but by the end it appears to be just like the movie where he just accepts who he is and the cracking of the stage and all just seemed to further enforce that he rejected it. There is no point in growing to accept and love ones self if your going to simply join into the instrumentality. To me the TV episodes were merely just more of the insights into the characters and all the crazy mind talk that happened during the instrumentality, though we get to see what the other characters had to say as they too would be part of the whole process in some form. To me both endings are one and just part of the whole. End of Eva simply put it all into perspective and gave it a narrative that made sense instead of a bunch of babble that had no direction previously.

[]:             Quote Post by Misato's toyboy » Sun Jun 07, 2009 5:08 pm SSD, I am thinking on your counterpoints. :) but AFA "farewell mother", I always took as saying farewell to an individual Shinji cared for, since Complementation will erase individualities. Not suicide, more the sacrificing of one state of existence, to achieve another. (Both Gunbuster & Gurren Lagann each have lifestyle & status quo trade-offs & sacrifices for the greater good & survival) My own theory for fitting EoE into the TV show is that it all "occurs", as an alternative Shinji-created-hell, reality before the end of 25, & is symbolised in 25 by the shattered spotlights. The images of Misato's & Ritsuko's corpses fit in as "shock" glimpses of what the outcome of Shinji's lashing-out reality was, & in the end scene of 25, Misato scolds Shinji, for creating that reality. In 26 he is "steered" to a more positive, pro-Complementation decision to reboot reality, which happens & the final scene of 26 is the conclusion of all original, pre-Rebuild Evangelion. To me Rebuild, is yet another Evaverse. Alternate realities are anime tradition for many re-writes, for many shows.

[]: Re: What people who shun eps. 25 & 26 are missing.             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Sun Jun 07, 2009 5:20 pm The flavour of Complementation does seem to differ between the two tellings; in particular Gendo's explanation in 25 about [code:1]Gendou: No, It's not that everything returns to nothingness. It's nothing but returning everything to the beginning. Nothing but returning things to the mother which has long since been lost. All the minds become one mind, obtaining peace forever. Nothing but that.[/code:1] doesn't really sit well with the autonomous hard take-off of EoE, and seems more like an explanation of something that Gendo has himself initiated in a controlled fashion (admittedly over the dead bodies of both Mistao and Ritsuko). Misato's toyboy wrote: Taking the ending to 26, & therefore Eva TV on its own, Shinji (seemingly) accepts Complementation.

[]:             Quote Post by Oedipusfoot » Sun Jun 07, 2009 6:31 pm **I'm usually overlooking a big detail (or the main point), so be gentle... Sailor Star Dust wrote: Actually just looking at EoTV as it's own ending, there's evidence that Shinji rejects it. Why would he/the Tel-op say "farewell mother" if Instrumentality is accepted? Why would he say only moments before the congratulations scene that he wants to be his own person and he wants to continue living, ...

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Sun Jun 07, 2009 7:24 pm Mr. Tines wrote: and seems more like an explanation of something that Gendo has himself initiated in a controlled fashion

[]:             Quote Post by Oedipusfoot » Sun Jun 07, 2009 7:36 pm Who wrote the Platinum commentaries?

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Sun Jun 07, 2009 9:15 pm Who translated them you mean? Not sure, other then somebody at ADV.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Sun Jun 07, 2009 11:25 pm I'm with Mr. Tines on this one (big suprise...) in saying that EoTV is the "Gendo Wins" scenario. Complementation and instrumentality still occur, but instead of going back after reaching the one-mind state, Shinji accepts himself regardless of where he is (as opposed to the it's not real tone of the movie), thus enabling him to a state of being in contact with others while still remaining himself. Thus the text at the end can be interpreted as "Thanks pop for actually doing soemthing that doesn't make my life suck, I don't need to hide behind your giant berserker skirt anymore mom, and if you'll excuse me I have a couple of friends/love interests to spend the next arbitrary amount of time hanging out with. Later!"

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Mon Jun 08, 2009 1:09 am Oedipusfoot wrote: @Mr. Tines: transcending complementation. That's very abstract. Would you go into some detail for minds not so sharp?

[]:             Quote Post by Zaque » Mon Jun 08, 2009 1:50 am NemZ wrote: I'm with Mr. Tines on this one (big suprise...) in saying that EoTV is the "Gendo Wins" scenario. Complementation and instrumentality still occur, but instead of going back after reaching the one-mind state, Shinji accepts himself regardless of where he is (as opposed to the it's not real tone of the movie), thus enabling him to a state of being in contact with others while still remaining himself.

[]:             Quote Post by Oedipusfoot » Mon Jun 08, 2009 4:15 am Zaque wrote: I don't think Gendo "wins" in either ending... then again, the two are more or less the same ending for me, or rather, EoE encompasses EoTV, hence the "one more final" at the end there.

[]:             Quote Post by Consul_Elect » Mon Jun 08, 2009 6:00 am i agree that the last 2 eps are important, they lend an insight to the characters development and conclusion to it. And it also embodies Anno's message about the true self and your perspective and reality. Which I think is an important message. And I myself have looked at it seriously and taken something away from it. But yes it does lack an ending to the plots.. which EoE provides. So i believe to to get the most out of the series you must watch both 'endings' as they are called. Otherwise you miss the whole message. I always thouth the ending of the TV show was Shinji wanting to live and not be intrumentalized.... So does that mean that the movie and TV ending are at odds with each other? Though these Rebuild movies are gonna screw everything up, if it really will provide a NEW ending. What will be the definitive ending when that happens??? Nooooo......

[]:             Quote Post by BattleMonkey » Mon Jun 08, 2009 7:48 am Rebuild is it's own universe, has no bearing on the Evangelion ending. As for TV versus EoE, EoE is supposed to be the true and final finale. If anything it pretty much overwrites the TV ending, though like others I don't like to ignore it as such as it features amazing character insight we don't see in EoE or anywhere else in the series.

[]:             Quote Post by Misato's toyboy » Mon Jun 08, 2009 1:08 pm Eva TV's timeline is different at the end. In the TV timeline, Gendou using Rei, immediately instigates 3rd impact following Shinji's defeat of Kaworu. He knows NERV is on borrowed time. In EoE, there is a delay as SEELE has hacked the Magi, which stalls Gendou - & maybe factors affect Rei's decision to turn against Gendou, most probably Rei's encounter with the angel (only seen/happened in the DC version) before she self-destructed in Eva 00. (OT I always think of Odo & The Dominion's unimind from ST S9 when considering Complemented humanity in Eva). The fact that Gendou wins his campaign, & reunites with Yui (also Misato reunites with Kaji), to me, is shown in the omedetou scene, where both couples are shown, together as couples. And all Shinji's friends, as well as parents, are also Complemented. Would TV Shinji not choose to be with them? Why reboot misery if all are happy being together under Complementation. Finally, the fact that Complementation has already taken place is reflected in the unified omedetou applause Shinji gets. The final scene is celebrating a fait accompli . The spotlights reassembling signify not only Shinji's mental healing, but his seal on Complementation, as the lights also represent the healing of Earth, reconiliation, & all being right.

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Mon Jun 08, 2009 1:27 pm Misato's Toyboy wrote: Eva TV's timeline is different at the end. In the TV timeline, Gendou using Rei, immediately instigates 3rd impact following Shinji's defeat of Kaworu. He knows NERV is on borrowed time.
